statement.markdown: You are given an array of integers. Your task is to find and output the largest element present in the array. The array size and its elements are provided via standard input. This classic problem tests basic array traversal and comparison operations.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer N, the number of elements in the array.
The second line contains N space-separated integers A1, A2, ..., AN.
statement.outputFormat: Print a single integer, the largest element among the given N integers.

constraints.description: Number of elements in the array

examples.explanation: The maximum value in the array is 5.

hints.text: Initialize a variable to store the largest element found so far.
Iterate through the array using a loop.
Compare each element with the current largest element.
If an element is larger, update the largest element variable.
After iterating through the entire array, output the largest element.

editorial.title: Linear Scan to Find the Largest Element
editorial.intuition: The task reduces to a single pass over the data: keep a running maximum and update it whenever a larger value appears. This simple observation turns a potentially quadratic or logarithmic approach into a linear one.
editorial.bruteForce: A naive solution would sort the array or compare every pair of elements, leading to O(N log N) or O(N^2) time. While correct, it wastes time and memory, especially for N up to 100,000.
editorial.betterSolution: Instead of sorting, we can maintain a variable holding the current maximum. As we read each number, we compare it to that variable and replace it if the new number is larger. This single loop uses only constant extra space.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal algorithm is a single linear scan that updates a running maximum. It runs in O(N) time and uses O(1) auxiliary space, matching the problem’s constraints.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Assume after processing the first k elements, the variable holds the maximum of those k elements. Initially, with k=1, this holds trivially. When the (k+1)-th element is processed, if it is larger than the current maximum, we replace it; otherwise we keep the current maximum. In both cases, the variable becomes the maximum of the first k+1 elements. By induction, after all N elements, the variable equals the maximum of the entire array.
editorial.dryRun: Example: array [3, -1, 7, 2]. Start max=3. Compare -1 → max stays 3. Compare 7 → max becomes 7. Compare 2 → max stays 7. Result: 7.
commonMistakes: Using max() on the entire list when the list is huge, causing unnecessary memory usage
Sorting the array and then picking the last element, which is O(N log N)
Assuming the array is non‑negative and initializing max to 0, missing negative values
Using a loop that compares each element with all others, leading to O(N^2)
Not handling the case when N=1 and returning the single element

solutions.python:
import sys
it = iter(sys.stdin.read().split())
n = int(next(it))
nums = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]
print(max(nums))
